hero.title: Stand taller, with less pain.

conditionsHeading.title: Common spine conditions we help with
conditionsHeading.lead: Back pain has many causes. Here are the conditions we most commonly support.

conditions.title: Lower Back Pain
conditions.description: Mechanical low back pain from poor posture, lifting strain, or muscle fatigue. Lumbar bracing offloads the spine and supports recovery.

conditions.title: Scoliosis
conditions.description: Curvature of the spine â€” often diagnosed in adolescence. Spinamic bracing has helped many patients reduce curve progression and avoid surgery.

conditions.title: Post-Surgical Spine Recovery
conditions.description: After discectomy, fusion, or endoscopic spine procedures, controlled support helps the spine heal in the right position.

conditions.title: Disc Herniation
conditions.description: Pinched nerves and radiating pain from a herniated disc â€” bracing reduces movement that aggravates symptoms during conservative care.

conditions.title: Postural Issues
conditions.description: Forward head posture, kyphosis, and rounded shoulders â€” postural braces serve as gentle reminders during daily activity.

conditions.title: Osteoporotic Compression
conditions.description: Vertebral compression fractures common in osteoporosis. Specialist bracing reduces pain and supports healing.

testimonials.name: Fawzul Hidhaya's Mother
testimonials.testimonial: My daughter was diagnosed with scoliosis at 11. After years of exercises, the curve reached 32Â°. Surgery was advised â€” but with Spinamic, before the brace it was 35Â°, and with the brace it reduced to 25Â°. We are very happy to have an alternative to surgery.

seoContent.title: Understanding back pain â€” and what helps
seoContent.intro: Back pain affects most adults at some point. Most cases resolve with conservative care, but knowing when bracing helps â€” and when to see a specialist â€” matters.

sections.heading: Why does my back hurt?
sections.body: Most back pain is mechanical â€” from posture, lifting, or muscle strain. Some pain comes from disc issues, scoliosis, or age-related changes. Identifying the cause matters because treatment varies.

sections.heading: How does spine bracing help?
sections.body: Bracing offloads stressed structures, limits painful motion, and gives the spine the right environment to heal. For scoliosis specifically, bracing during growth has strong evidence for slowing curve progression.

sections.heading: When should I see a doctor?
sections.body: If back pain is severe, lasts more than a few weeks, radiates into your legs, or is accompanied by numbness, weakness, or changes in bladder control â€” see a spine specialist immediately.

seoContent.disclaimer: This page is educational. Spine conditions vary widely. Please consult a qualified spine specialist before starting any bracing programme.

faq.question: How long does scoliosis bracing take to show results?
faq.answer: Most studies on scoliosis bracing show meaningful curve stabilisation or reduction over 12â€“24 months of consistent wear during the active growth period. Results depend heavily on age, curve type, and brace-wear compliance.

faq.question: Can I sit at work with a lumbar brace?
faq.answer: Yes â€” most lumbar bracing is designed for daily use including seated work. Your specialist will recommend wear duration based on your specific condition.

faq.question: Will wearing a brace weaken my back muscles?
faq.answer: This is a common concern, but research shows it is largely a myth when bracing is paired with appropriate physiotherapy. Bracing supports recovery; rehabilitation strengthens the back.
